segments.caption: The video opens with a close-up view of a Spiral Galaxy, showcasing its bright, swirling core and dark dust lanes, emphasizing its grand structure. The scene transitions to introduce two prominent astronomers: Vera C. Rubin (1928–2016) and William Kent Ford Jr. (1931–Present), with their portraits displayed against the cosmic backdrop. The focus then shifts to a simplified, rotating model of a galaxy, illustrating its disk structure and outlying stars, with a label pointing to the 'OUTLYING STARS'. The narrative progresses to explain Newton’s Inverse-Square Law, displaying the formula and a graph showing how gravitational force diminishes with distance. This is followed by a velocity curve graph comparing the 'Expected from the visible disk' (yellow dashed line) with the 'Actual observations' (blue dashed line), revealing a discrepancy that suggests unseen mass. The video uses visual animations of orbiting stars to demonstrate the expected and observed rotational speeds, culminating in the revelation that stars at the galaxy’s edge move faster than predicted, hinting at the presence of dark matter.

segments.caption: The video begins with a graph titled 'Graph of Expected vs. Actual Rotational Velocities,' displaying two lines: a yellow dashed line labeled 'Expected from the visible disk' and a blue dashed line labeled 'Actual observations,' plotting velocity (V in km/s) against a star's distance from the galactic core (in light-years). The graph is overlaid on a dark, starry background that gradually transitions into a detailed, rotating spiral galaxy, viewed from above, with a bright core and swirling arms. The camera then zooms out, revealing the galaxy from an edge-on perspective, showcasing its flat, luminous disk against the dark void of space. The view continues to pull back, showing the galaxy as a small, distant spiral among countless stars, emphasizing its isolation in the cosmos. The scene shifts to a black-and-white, grainy depiction of the galaxy, followed by a transition to a scientific paper titled 'The Rotation of Spiral Galaxies' by Vera C. Rubin, published in Science on June 24, 1983. The abstract highlights evidence that up to 90% of the universe's mass is nonluminous and clumped around galaxies, with dark matter's gravitational force explaining the high rotational velocities of stars and gas. The video concludes with a return to the spiral galaxy, now shown in full color and rotating slowly, surrounded by a sparse starfield, reinforcing the theme of dark matter's unseen influence on galactic structure.

segments.caption: The video begins with a cosmic backdrop of a starfield and a glowing nebula-like band, setting a deep-space atmosphere. The text 'MOND' appears in bold white letters, followed by its full name 'MODIFIED NEWTONIAN DYNAMICS' beneath it. A portrait of Mordehai 'Moti' Milgrom (1946–Present) is displayed, introducing the theorist behind the concept. The scene transitions to a mathematical equation illustrating MOND’s core principle: at an acceleration threshold of 1.2×10⁻¹⁰ m/s², gravitational force is modified, leading to the relation v⁴ = GMa₀. A graph then appears, plotting gravitational force against distance, showing a blue curve labeled 'MOND Decay' that levels off at large distances, contrasting with a red curve labeled 'Newtonian Inverse-Square Decay' that continues to decline. This is followed by a second graph plotting rotational velocity (V) against a star’s distance from the galactic core, where a green dashed line labeled 'MOND' closely matches the blue dashed line labeled 'Dark Matter', both deviating from the yellow dashed line labeled 'Newtonian Stars & Gas'. The video concludes with a rotating spiral galaxy, then a glowing orange nebula-like structure, symbolizing the cosmic scale of MOND’s implications.
